title: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms
authors: Neeraj K. Surana, Dennis L. Kasper

items.subtopic: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms | HISTORIC PERSPECTIVE
items.title: Role of Anaerobes in Polymicrobial Infections
items.content: Anaerobes are a predominant class of bacteria in the normal human microbiota, often involved in infections arising from mucosal surfaces. These infections typically become apparent when there is a breakdown of mucosal barriers, allowing the microbiota to enter sterile sites and cause polymicrobial infections involving both aerobic and anaerobic bacteria.
keyPoints: Anaerobes are predominant in normal human microbiota on mucous membranes.
Infections often occur after mucosal barrier breakdown leading to leakage into sterile sites.
Polymicrobial nature involves both aerobic and anaerobic bacteria.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms | HISTORIC PERSPECTIVE

items.subtopic: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms | HISTORIC PERSPECTIVE
items.title: Pathogenesis of Anaerobic Infections
items.content: Anaerobic infections develop in environments where oxygen is depleted, typically within closed spaces. This occurs as aerobic organisms consume available oxygen, creating conditions that favor the growth of anaerobic bacteria.
keyPoints: Anaerobic environment development due to oxygen consumption by aerobic organisms.
Outgrowth of anaerobes in closed spaces with limited oxygen.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms | HISTORIC PERSPECTIVE

items.subtopic: DIFFERENCES BETWEEN ANAEROBIC AND AEROBIC ORGANISMS
items.title: Oxygen Tolerance and Enzymatic Defense in Anaerobic Organisms
items.content: Anaerobic organisms, including clinically relevant ones like Bacteroides fragilis, have varying tolerances to oxygen. Obligate anaerobes are killed by ≥0.5% oxygen, while aerotolerant organisms can tolerate but not utilize it for growth. The ability of these organisms to withstand oxidative stress is partly due to enzymes such as superoxide dismutase and catalase.
keyPoints: Obligate anaerobes are killed by ≥0.5% oxygen.
Aerotolerant organisms can tolerate but not use oxygen for growth.
Enzymatic defenses like superoxide dismutase and catalase help in tolerating oxygen.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · DIFFERENCES BETWEEN ANAEROBIC AND AEROBIC ORGANISMS

items.subtopic: DIFFERENCES BETWEEN ANAEROBIC AND AEROBIC ORGANISMS
items.title: Energy Metabolism Differences Between Aerobic and Anaerobic Organisms
items.content: Aerobic organisms rely on oxygen as the final electron acceptor in their respiration, making it a highly efficient process. In contrast, anaerobic organisms use less oxidizing agents like sulfate or nitrate for anaerobic respiration, resulting in lower ATP yield. Fermentation, another anaerobic pathway, is even less efficient than both aerobic and anaerobic respiration, yielding only ~5% of the energy compared to aerobic processes.
keyPoints: Aerobic respiration uses oxygen as the final electron acceptor.
Anaerobic respiration is less efficient due to less oxidizing final electron acceptors.
Fermentation yields ~5% of the energy compared to aerobic respiration.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · DIFFERENCES BETWEEN ANAEROBIC AND AEROBIC ORGANISMS

items.title: Anaerobic Dominance in Human Microbiota
items.content: The human microbiota, particularly on mucocutaneous surfaces, is predominantly composed of anaerobic bacteria. These organisms are crucial for maintaining the balance and health of these environments.
keyPoints: Anaerobes account for 99.0–99.9% of cultivable microbiota in many human body sites.
Concentration ranges from $10^{3}/mL$ in the nose to $10^{12}/mL$ in gingival scrapings and the colon.
The oral cavity has a high anaerobe-to-aerobe ratio, reaching 1000:1 in gingival crevices.
Anaerobic bacteria are not found in appreciable numbers in the normal stomach and proximal small intestine.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · ANAEROBES OF THE HUMAN MICROBIOTA

items.title: Key Anaerobic Species Across Human Sites
items.content: Specific anaerobic species are predominant across various human body sites, contributing to the unique microbiota composition of each location.
keyPoints: Prevotella and Porphyromonas dominate the oral anaerobic microbiota.
The female genital tract hosts ~$10^{9}$ organisms/mL with a 10:1 anaerobe-to-aerobe ratio, including Prevotella, Bacteroides, Fusobacterium, Clostridium, and Lactobacillus species.
Cutibacterium acnes is the predominant anaerobic species on the skin.
The human gut harbors >1000 bacterial species, with 100–200 species in any given individual.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · ANAEROBES OF THE HUMAN MICROBIOTA

items.title: Role of Anaerobes in Nutrient Metabolism and Immune Modulation
items.content: Anaerobic bacteria, particularly Bacteroides species, are crucial for metabolizing dietary carbohydrates into usable forms such as polysaccharides. They also produce essential secreted products like vitamin K and bile acids that aid in fat absorption and cholesterol regulation. Additionally, these anaerobes play a significant role in immune modulation by promoting resistance to pathogenic colonization and regulating the host's immune response.
keyPoints: Bacteroides species express 172 glycosyl hydrolases for polysaccharide degradation.
Anaerobic microbiota produces vitamin K and bile acids.
Promotes resistance to pathogens like Salmonella enterica serotype Typhimurium and Clostridium difficile.
Immunomodulatory properties include balancing T cell functions and inducing regulatory T cells via polysaccharide A (PSA).
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · ANAEROBES AND HUMAN HEALTH

items.title: Impact of Dysregulated Gut Microbiota on Disease Pathogenesis
items.content: Dysregulation of the gut microbiota is implicated in various diseases characterized by inflammation and aberrant immune responses. Conditions such as inflammatory bowel disease, rheumatoid arthritis, multiple sclerosis, asthma, and type 1 diabetes may be influenced by changes in the gut microbiome. Additionally, there is an association between gut microbiota dysregulation and metabolic conditions like obesity and metabolic syndrome.
keyPoints: Dysregulation of gut microbiota linked to inflammatory diseases (e.g., IBD, rheumatoid arthritis).
Associated with aberrant immune responses in multiple sclerosis, asthma, type 1 diabetes.
Connection between gut microbiota and obesity/metabolic syndrome.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · ANAEROBES AND HUMAN HEALTH

items.title: Understanding Anaerobic Infections: Disruption of Host-Microbiota Harmony
items.content: Anaerobic infections occur when the normal balance between the host and its microbiota is disturbed, allowing anaerobes to invade sterile tissues. This can happen through mucosal disruptions or direct inoculation via trauma.
keyPoints: Over 10,000 species of bacteria in human microbiota are primarily anaerobic.
Anaerobic infections often result from the introduction of these organisms into otherwise sterile tissue.
Common sites for infection include areas colonized by specific anaerobes such as oro- and nasopharyngeal regions.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · ETIOLOGY

items.title: Key Anaerobic Genera in Human Infections: Site-Specific Pathogens
items.content: Certain genera of anaerobes are commonly isolated from infections, reflecting the microbiota of neighboring sites. For instance, oro- and nasopharyngeal microbes can cause odontogenic or pleuropulmonary infections.
keyPoints: Commonly isolated anaerobic genera include Bacteroides fragilis group (e.g., B. vulgatus, B. thetaiotaomicron) from intraabdominal abscesses.
Anaerobic gram-negative bacilli like Prevotella and Fusobacterium are significant pathogens.
Clostridium species are primary spore-forming anaerobic rods causing human disease.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · ETIOLOGY

items.title: Anaerobic Environment Requirements
items.content: Anaerobic infections necessitate an environment with a lowered oxidation-reduction potential, which can occur in tissue ischemia, trauma, surgery, or perforated viscus. Facultative organisms often maintain this environment to support obligate anaerobes.
keyPoints: Lowered oxidation-reduction potential is essential for anaerobic infections.
Facultative organisms help maintain conditions favorable for obligate anaerobes.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · ■ PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.title: Virulence Factors of Anaerobes
items.content: Anaerobic bacteria express virulence factors that aid in evading host defenses, adhering to surfaces, and producing toxins. Notably, B. fragilis has a capsular polysaccharide complex crucial for abscess formation.
keyPoints: Virulence factors include evasion of host defenses, adhesion, toxin production, and surface structures like capsular polysaccharides.
B. fragilis's capsular polysaccharide is central to its virulence in intraabdominal infections.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · ■ PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.title: Role of B. fragilis Capsular Polysaccharides
items.content: B. fragilis produces multiple capsular polysaccharides, with PSA and PSB inducing abscesses through cytokine stimulation via Toll-like receptor 2.
keyPoints: B. fragilis can produce at least eight distinct capsular polysaccharides.
PSA induces abscess formation by stimulating macrophages to release IL-8, IL-17, and TNF-$\alpha$ through a Toll-like receptor 2-dependent mechanism.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · ■ PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: ■ PATHOGENESIS (part 2) | Infections Due to Anaerobic Bacteria
items.title: Synergistic Pathogenesis of Anaerobic Infections
items.content: Anaerobic infections often involve multiple organisms working synergistically, typically three to six different species. This synergy is facilitated by various mechanisms such as creating a favorable growth environment, inhibiting host defenses, providing necessary growth factors for other organisms, and causing tissue damage that promotes infection spread.
keyPoints: Infections typically involve 3-6 different organisms due to synergistic interactions.

Synergies include creation of anaerobic environments, inhibition of phagocytes by short-chain fatty acids and succinic acid, provision of growth factors like vitamin K for P. melaninogenica, and tissue damage promoting infection spread.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · ■ PATHOGENESIS (part 2) | Infections Due to Anaerobic Bacteria

items.subtopic: ■ PATHOGENESIS (part 2) | Infections Due to Anaerobic Bacteria
items.title: Diagnosis and Management of Anaerobic Infections
items.content: Anaerobic infections are often indicated by the presence of multiple organisms in abscesses, failure of routine cultures, and specific clinical signs. Gas production is common but not diagnostic. A putrid smell is a late sign present in 30-50% of cases. Effective treatment involves antibiotics targeting some organisms combined with debridement and drainage.
keyPoints: Anaerobic infections often involve >10 species in supportive sites.
Abscesses failing routine cultures likely contain anaerobes; Gram’s stain reveals bacteria in 'sterile pus'.
Gas presence is common but not diagnostic for anaerobic infections.
Putrid smell develops late, present in 30-50% of cases.
Effective treatment includes antibiotics and debridement to disrupt bacterial synergy.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · ■ PATHOGENESIS (part 2) | Infections Due to Anaerobic Bacteria

items.title: Prevalence and Common Types of Anaerobic Infections
items.content: Anaerobic infections are frequently encountered, with anaerobes constituting a significant portion of bacteria isolated from both inpatients and outpatients. Bacteremia and soft tissue infections are the most common types.
keyPoints: Anaerobes constitute 7–8% of bacteria isolated from inpatients and 13–15% from outpatients.
Typically, anaerobic bacteria account for <1% of all cases of bacteremia.
Bacteremia and soft tissue infections are the most common types of anaerobic infection.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.title: Characteristics and Clinical Findings in Anaerobic Infections
items.content: Anaerobic infections often present with specific clinical findings such as abscess formation, putrid purulence, septic thrombophlebitis, tissue necrosis, and resistance to broad-spectrum antibiotics lacking anaerobic activity.
keyPoints: Common characteristics include abscess formation, putrid purulence, septic thrombophlebitis, tissue necrosis.
Anaerobes may not respond to broad-spectrum antibiotics that lack activity against them.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.title: Acute Necrotizing Ulcerative Gingivitis and Noma
items.content: Acute necrotizing ulcerative gingivitis can progress to noma, a severe gangrenous infection. It is most common in young children with immune dysfunction due to malnutrition.
keyPoints: Necrotizing infections like Vincent's stomatitis present with painful bleeding gums and foul breath.
Noma occurs primarily in children aged 1–4 years, especially those with malnutrition-related immune dysfunction.
Incidence of noma is 1–7 cases per 1000 children in sub-Saharan Africa.
Mortality rate without treatment is 70–90%.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.title: Anaerobic Bacteria in Chronic Sinusitis
items.content: Anaerobic bacteria are prevalent in chronic sinusitis, found in two-thirds of patients and often exceeding 90% in studies. They constitute about 40% of all cultured bacteria from these infections.
keyPoints: Anaerobic bacteria present in >90% of chronic sinusitis cases
~40% of all bacteria cultured are anaerobes
Commonly isolated anaerobes: Peptostreptococcus, Prevotella, Porphyromonas
Aerobic counterparts: S. aureus and Enterobacteriaceae
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.title: Complications from Anaerobic Head and Neck Infections
items.content: Anaerobic infections can extend to contiguous areas, leading to severe complications like osteomyelitis, brain abscesses, subdural empyema, mediastinitis, pleuropulmonary infection, and Lemierre's syndrome.
keyPoints: Cranial spread can cause skull or mandible osteomyelitis
Intracranial infections: brain abscess (up to 30%), subdural empyema (10%)
Lemierre’s syndrome involves F. necrophorum, with septic thrombophlebitis of the internal jugular vein
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.title: Anaerobic Infections in Pleuropulmonary Conditions
items.content: Anaerobes like Prevotella and Veillonella, along with oral microaerophilic streptococci, can cause pleuropulmonary infections such as aspiration pneumonia or lung abscess in patients with impaired clearance or increased aspiration.
keyPoints: Anaerobic bacteria include Prevotella and Veillonella species
Associated with conditions like aspiration pneumonia and lung abscess
Indolent course differentiates from acute conditions like pneumococcal pneumonia
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.title: Distinguishing Bacterial Aspiration Pneumonia from Other Aspiration Syndromes
items.content: Bacterial aspiration pneumonia develops over days to weeks, whereas chemical pneumonitis and foreign body obstruction occur rapidly. Key differentiators include the presence of fever, malaise, sputum production, and specific radiographic findings.
keyPoints: Chemical pneumonitis (e.g., Mendelson syndrome) occurs within 4–6 hours post-aspiration, often without fever but with rapid pulmonary inflammation leading to hypoxia. Chest x-ray may show a 'whiteout' in 8-24 hours.
Bacterial aspiration pneumonia presents over days/weeks with fever, malaise, sputum production; chest x-rays reveal consolidation in dependent lung segments.
Antibiotic therapy for chemical pneumonitis is unnecessary unless bacterial superinfection occurs.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 3)

items.title: Clinical Presentation and Diagnosis of Anaerobic Lung Abscesses
items.content: Anaerobic lung abscesses are characterized by subacute symptoms such as malaise, weight loss, fever, night sweats, and foul-smelling sputum over 1-3 weeks. They often involve multiple dependent pulmonary segment cavities.
keyPoints: Symptoms include constitutional signs (malaise, weight loss, fever) lasting 1–3 weeks prior to hospitalization.
Abscesses are typically in dependent lung segments and may be single or multiple.
~90% of cases have anaerobic isolates identified, often with concurrent aerobic organisms like S. aureus.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 3)

items.title: Understanding Empyema in the Context of Anaerobic Pulmonary Infections
items.content: Empyema results from chronic anaerobic pulmonary infections, presenting with thick purulent material. It is a late manifestation requiring specific antibiotic coverage.
keyPoints: Empyema signifies long-standing infection with thick, purulent material.
Anaerobic coverage in antibiotics is crucial for clinical improvement.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 3)

items.title: Differentiating Empyema from Parapneumonic Effusion
items.content: Empyema must be distinguished from parapneumonic effusions, which are typically caused by common pneumonia pathogens like S. pneumoniae. The differentiation is based on the characteristics of pleural fluid.
keyPoints: Empyema presents with foul-smelling thick pus and a mean WBC count of ~55,000 cells/mL, lactate dehydrogenase level >1000 IU/L, and pH <7.2.
Parapneumonic effusion has a thin exudate with a mean WBC count of ~5000 cells/mL, lactate dehydrogenase level >200 IU/L, and pH ~7.4.
Empyema may require drainage or decortication for resolution.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 4)

items.title: Characteristics of Neutropenic Enterocolitis
items.content: Neutropenic enterocolitis, or typhlitis, is characterized by bowel wall thickening in neutropenic patients, often following chemotherapy.
keyPoints: Bowel wall thickening typically exceeds 4 mm.
Occurs after 1–2 weeks of chemotherapy-induced neutropenia.
>5% of adults hospitalized for malignancy may develop typhlitis.
Right lower quadrant pain is common but absent in nearly half of cases.
Clostridium septicum bacteremia indicates severe disease and possible colon cancer.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 4)

items.title: Anaerobic Involvement in Pelvic Infections
items.content: Pelvic infections often involve anaerobes, which can be part of mixed etiologies with coliforms.
keyPoints: Major anaerobic pathogens include P. bivia, P. disiens, and B. fragilis group.
Anaerobes are frequently involved in pelvic inflammatory disease, abscesses, endometritis, tubo-ovarian abscesses, septic abortion, and postoperative infections.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 4)

items.title: Characteristics and Management of Gas Gangrene
items.content: Gas gangrene, often caused by C. perfringens, involves extensive gas formation in tissues leading to crepitus and a thin, dark discharge. It presents with fever and tenderness, rapidly spreading if untreated.
keyPoints: Mostly due to C. perfringens
Characterized by extensive tissue gas formation
Presents with fever and tenderness
Rapid spread requires prompt treatment
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 5)

items.title: Distinguishing Features of Necrotizing Fasciitis
items.content: Necrotizing fasciitis can be caused by group A streptococci or a mix of anaerobes and aerobes. Polymicrobial forms progress over 3-5 days with symptoms like fever, subcutaneous gas, anesthesia before skin necrosis, and foul-smelling discharge.
keyPoints: Progression over 3–5 days in polymicrobial cases
Symptoms: fever, subcutaneous gas, anesthesia, foul odor
Causative agents suggested by specific symptoms (e.g., B. fragilis group with lymphadenopathy)
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 5)

items.title: Anaerobic Infections in Bone and Joints: Actinomycosis
items.content: Actinomyces species are the primary cause of anaerobic bone infections, with cervicofacial actinomycosis presenting as a 'lumpy jaw' due to soft tissue swelling. These chronic infections may form sinus tracts and require prolonged antibiotic treatment.
keyPoints: Most common in the mandible
Characterized by 'lumpy jaw'
Chronic nature with potential for sinus tract development
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 5)

items.title: Mechanisms of Anaerobic Osteomyelitis Development
items.content: Anaerobic osteomyelitis often develops by extension from adjacent infections, such as soft tissue or sinus infections. Hematogenous seeding is uncommon and occurs in fewer than 10% of cases.
keyPoints: Commonly extends from adjacent infections (e.g., diabetic foot ulcers).
Hematogenous seeding is rare (<10%).
Most common sites: head (skull, jaw) and extremities.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 6)

items.title: Characteristics of Anaerobic Septic Arthritis
items.content: Anaerobic arthritis is uncommon and typically involves a single isolate. It often results from hematogenous spread, with C. acnes, peptostreptococci, and B. fragilis being frequent causes.
keyPoints: Uncommon, usually involves a single isolate.
<5% of B. fragilis bacteremia cases result in arthritis.
C. acnes and peptostreptococci associated with prosthetic joints.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 6)

items.title: Bacteremia Due to B. fragilis
items.content: B. fragilis is the most common anaerobe in blood cultures, often indicating colonic pathology. Mortality rates are high if untreated, with variations based on species and patient factors.
keyPoints: Most common anaerobic pathogen in blood cultures.
0.8% of positive U.S. blood cultures yield an anaerobic gram-negative bacillus.
Untreated B. fragilis bacteremia has a 60% mortality rate.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 6)

items.title: Critical Steps for Diagnosing Anaerobic Infections
items.content: Diagnosis of anaerobic infections relies on proper specimen collection, rapid transport in anaerobic media, and appropriate laboratory handling. Specimens from normally sterile sites are preferred to avoid contamination by normal microbiota.
keyPoints: Proper collection involves meticulous sampling without contamination.
Use liquid or tissue specimens; if swabs are necessary, use special anaerobic systems.
Liquid samples should be collected in air-free syringes and transported quickly.
Specimens from normally sterile sites (e.g., blood, pleural fluid) are appropriate for culture.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Presumptive Diagnosis of Anaerobic Infections
items.content: Diagnosis often relies on presumptive evidence due to the difficulty in isolating anaerobes. Clinical factors like abscess presence, foul odor, and gas in tissues suggest anaerobic involvement.
keyPoints: Anaerobic infections suggested by clinical factors: abscess, foul odor, tissue gas.
If cultures yield no growth or only aerobes with mixed Gram stain, suspect anaerobes.
Prior antibiotic therapy can reduce cultivability of anaerobes.
Lack of response to non-anaerobic antibiotics (e.g., aminoglycosides) suggests anaerobic etiology.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: Anaerobic Infections | ANTIBIOTIC THERAPY AND RESISTANCE (part 1)
items.title: Empirical Antibiotic Selection for Anaerobic Infections
items.content: Selecting empirical antibiotics for anaerobic infections should be based on the infection's location, severity, and local resistance patterns. The choice of antibiotic must consider both aerobic and anaerobic organisms due to mixed etiology.
keyPoints: Empirical therapy is often sufficient for polymicrobial infections involving anaerobes.
Antibiotics with no activity against anaerobes include aminoglycosides, monobactams, and trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole.
Common empirical agents: metronidazole, β-lactam/β-lactamase inhibitor combinations, clindamycin, carbapenems, chloramphenicol.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · Anaerobic Infections | ANTIBIOTIC THERAPY AND RESISTANCE (part 1)

items.subtopic: Anaerobic Infections | ANTIBIOTIC THERAPY AND RESISTANCE (part 1)
items.title: Metronidazole Use and Resistance in Anaerobic Therapy
items.content: Metronidazole is a first-line agent for many anaerobic infections but has limitations. It is effective against gram-negative anaerobes like Bacteroides species, yet resistance rates are rising.
keyPoints: Metronidazole is active against nearly all Bacteroides species and Clostridium species.
Resistance to metronidazole in Bacteroides has been increasing in Europe.
In Colombia, 45% of Fusobacterium isolates from refractory periodontitis were resistant.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · Anaerobic Infections | ANTIBIOTIC THERAPY AND RESISTANCE (part 1)

items.subtopic: Anaerobic Infections | ANTIBIOTIC THERAPY AND RESISTANCE (part 1)
items.title: Resistance Patterns and Susceptibility Testing for Anaerobes
items.content: Antibiotic susceptibility testing is crucial due to increasing resistance rates, geographic variability, and species-specific profiles. It's particularly important when infections are serious or refractory.
keyPoints: Susceptibility testing should be considered for serious infections like brain abscess, meningitis, joint infection.
Resistance to piperacillin-tazobactam remains low (≤1%) in the U.S. but is 17% in Korea for B. thetaiotaomicron.
Clindamycin resistance has increased to 30-43% in the U.S., and >80% globally.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · Anaerobic Infections | ANTIBIOTIC THERAPY AND RESISTANCE (part 1)

items.title: Understanding Clindamycin Resistance Trends
items.content: Clindamycin is effective against many anaerobes, but resistance among Bacteroides species has significantly increased in the U.S. from 7% in 1981 to 33% by 2010–2012.
keyPoints: Resistance rates for clindamycin among Bacteroides species rose from 7% (1981) to 33% (2010-2012).
Clindamycin resistance is less common (<10%) in non-Bacteroides gram-negative anaerobes.
Some Clostridium species are resistant, but C. perfringens typically is not.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · ANTIBIOTIC THERAPY AND RESISTANCE (part 2)

items.title: Carbapenems as Effective Agents Against Anaerobes
items.content: Carbapenems, including imipenem, are highly effective against anaerobic infections with low resistance rates among Bacteroides isolates.
keyPoints: Resistance in Bacteroides isolates to carbapenems is below 3%.
Imipenem generally has the lowest resistance rate among carbapenems.
Carbapenems are effective despite $\beta$-lactamase production by most Bacteroides species, except for rare carbapenemase-producing B. fragilis strains.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · ANTIBIOTIC THERAPY AND RESISTANCE (part 2)

items.title: Chloramphenicol Use in Anaerobic Infections
items.content: While chloramphenicol has excellent in vitro activity against anaerobes, its clinical use is limited due to documented failures and should be considered when other options are unavailable.
keyPoints: Resistance to chloramphenicol is rare among Bacteroides species.
Elevated MICs (e.g., 16 $\mu$g/mL) have been noted in some isolates, indicating potential resistance.
Clinical failures with chloramphenicol suggest it should be a secondary option if other effective agents are available.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · ANTIBIOTIC THERAPY AND RESISTANCE (part 2)

items.subtopic: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms | HISTORIC PERSPECTIVE
items.question: Who first identified anaerobic organisms and what was the surprising observation made?
options: Antonie van Leeuwenhoek observed 'animalcules' in both open and sealed tubes, noting larger bacteria in the sealed tube.
Louis Pasteur discovered anaerobic organisms by observing bacterial growth in oxygen-free environments.
Robert Koch identified anaerobes using a special culture medium that excluded air.
Alexander Fleming found anaerobic bacteria while studying penicillin's effects on microbial cultures.
items.explanation: Antonie van Leeuwenhoek first identified anaerobic organisms and observed larger 'animalcules' in the sealed tube compared to the open one.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms | HISTORIC PERSPECTIVE

items.subtopic: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms | HISTORIC PERSPECTIVE
items.question: What principle underlies the basic pathogenesis of anaerobic infections as described by Leeuwenhoek?
options: Anaerobic organisms thrive in oxygen-rich environments.
Development of an anaerobic environment occurs due to oxygen consumption by aerobic organisms, allowing anaerobes to outgrow.
Anaerobic bacteria are primarily found on mucosal surfaces without any external influence.
The presence of aerobic bacteria prevents the growth of anaerobic organisms.
items.explanation: The development of an anaerobic environment in a closed space due to oxygen consumption by aerobic organisms allows for the outgrowth of anaerobes.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms | HISTORIC PERSPECTIVE

items.subtopic: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms | HISTORIC PERSPECTIVE
items.question: When were Leeuwenhoek's findings on anaerobic organisms confirmed?
options: Shortly after 1680 by contemporaries of Leeuwenhoek.
In the mid- to late nineteenth century by Pasteur and others.
During the early twentieth century with advancements in microbiology.
items.explanation: Leeuwenhoek’s findings on anaerobic organisms were confirmed in the mid- to late nineteenth century by Pasteur and others.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms | HISTORIC PERSPECTIVE

items.subtopic: DIFFERENCES BETWEEN ANAEROBIC AND AEROBIC ORGANISMS
items.question: Which of the following organisms is an example of a clinically relevant aerotolerant anaerobe?
items.explanation: Bacteroides fragilis is an example of a clinically relevant aerotolerant anaerobe.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · DIFFERENCES BETWEEN ANAEROBIC AND AEROBIC ORGANISMS

items.subtopic: DIFFERENCES BETWEEN ANAEROBIC AND AEROBIC ORGANISMS
items.question: What is the primary reason that aerobic respiration produces more ATP per glucose molecule than anaerobic respiration?
options: Oxygen acts as a final electron acceptor in aerobic respiration.
Anaerobic organisms do not use an electrochemical gradient.
Aerobic respiration does not require oxygen.
items.explanation: Oxygen acts as a final electron acceptor in aerobic respiration, making it more efficient than anaerobic respiration.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · DIFFERENCES BETWEEN ANAEROBIC AND AEROBIC ORGANISMS

items.subtopic: DIFFERENCES BETWEEN ANAEROBIC AND AEROBIC ORGANISMS
items.question: Which of the following is NOT true about facultative anaerobes?
options: They can grow in both the presence and absence of oxygen.
They preferentially utilize oxygen if it is available.
They rely on fermentation as their primary energy source.
They can switch to anaerobic respiration when oxygen is limited.
items.explanation: Facultative anaerobes do not primarily rely on fermentation; they prefer aerobic respiration if oxygen is available and use anaerobic respiration otherwise.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · DIFFERENCES BETWEEN ANAEROBIC AND AEROBIC ORGANISMS

items.question: Which area of the human body has the highest concentration of anaerobic bacteria?
items.explanation: The passage states that concentrations range up to $10^{12}/mL$ in gingival scrapings and the colon.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · ANAEROBES OF THE HUMAN MICROBIOTA

items.question: Which of the following species is predominantly found in the indigenous oral anaerobic microbiota?
items.explanation: The passage mentions that Prevotella and Porphyromonas species make up much of the indigenous oral anaerobic microbiota.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · ANAEROBES OF THE HUMAN MICROBIOTA

items.question: In which part of the gastrointestinal tract are anaerobic bacteria not found in appreciable numbers?
options: Normal stomach and proximal small intestine
items.explanation: The passage states that anaerobic bacteria are not found in appreciable numbers in the normal stomach and proximal small intestine.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · ANAEROBES OF THE HUMAN MICROBIOTA

items.question: Which organism within the intestines expresses the widest array of polysaccharide-degrading enzymes?
items.explanation: Bacteroides species express the widest array of polysaccharide-degrading enzymes.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · ANAEROBES AND HUMAN HEALTH

items.question: What is one way that the normal intestinal microbiota promotes resistance to colonization by pathogenic bacteria?
options: By increasing oxygen levels in the gut
Through depletion of oxygen and nutrients
By producing more glucose for pathogens
By reducing the host's immune response
items.explanation: The normal intestinal microbiota promotes resistance by depleting oxygen and nutrients.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · ANAEROBES AND HUMAN HEALTH

items.question: Which of the following diseases is associated with dysregulation of the gut microbiota?
items.explanation: Dysregulation of the gut microbiota is associated with inflammatory bowel disease.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · ANAEROBES AND HUMAN HEALTH

items.question: Which organisms are most commonly isolated from female genital tract infections?
options: P. melaninogenica, Fusobacterium necrophorum, F. nucleatum
items.explanation: Prevotella bivia and Prevotella disiens are the most common isolates in female genital tract infections.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · ETIOLOGY

items.question: Which group of organisms is among the most frequently isolated from clinical infections due to anaerobic bacteria?
items.explanation: The B. fragilis group, which includes multiple species like B. thetaiotaomicron and B. vulgatus, is frequently isolated from clinical infections.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · ETIOLOGY

items.question: Which of the following anaerobic bacteria are part of the oral microbiota and implicated in human disease?
items.explanation: Veillonella species are among the few anaerobic gram-negative cocci implicated in human disease and part of the oral microbiota.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · ETIOLOGY

items.question: Which of the following conditions is NOT directly associated with creating an anaerobic environment conducive to infections?
items.explanation: Hyperoxia increases oxygen levels, which is not conducive to an anaerobic environment.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · ■ PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.question: What role do facultative organisms play in polymicrobial infections involving obligate anaerobes?
options: They maintain a lowered oxidation-reduction potential.
They form abscesses as a protective measure.
items.explanation: Facultative organisms help maintain a low oxidation-reduction potential, allowing obligate anaerobes to thrive.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · ■ PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.question: Which virulence factor of B. fragilis is primarily responsible for abscess formation in intraabdominal infections?
items.explanation: The capsular polysaccharide complex of B. fragilis is the major virulence factor for abscess formation.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · ■ PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.question: What structural feature of PSA contributes to its ability to induce abscesses?
items.explanation: The zwitterionic charge motif in PSA is crucial for its ability to induce abscesses.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · ■ PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: ■ PATHOGENESIS (part 2) | Infections Due to Anaerobic Bacteria
items.question: Which of the following is a virulence factor that aids in the survival of bystander organisms by inhibiting phagocytes?
options: Production of short-chain fatty acids and succinic acid
items.explanation: Short-chain fatty acids and succinic acid inhibit phagocytes, aiding in the survival of bystander organisms.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · ■ PATHOGENESIS (part 2) | Infections Due to Anaerobic Bacteria

items.subtopic: ■ PATHOGENESIS (part 2) | Infections Due to Anaerobic Bacteria
items.question: What is a common clinical feature of anaerobic infections?
options: Presence of gas in all cases
Typically involving three to six different organisms
items.explanation: Anaerobic infections typically involve three to six different organisms.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · ■ PATHOGENESIS (part 2) | Infections Due to Anaerobic Bacteria

items.subtopic: ■ PATHOGENESIS (part 2) | Infections Due to Anaerobic Bacteria
items.question: What is a clue that an abscess might contain anaerobic bacteria?
options: Yielding multiple organisms on routine culture
Failure to yield organisms on routine culture
items.explanation: The failure of an abscess to yield organisms on routine culture suggests it may contain anaerobic bacteria.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · ■ PATHOGENESIS (part 2) | Infections Due to Anaerobic Bacteria

items.question: Which of the following is the most common type of anaerobic infection?
items.explanation: The passage states that bacteremia and soft tissue infections are the most common types of anaerobic infection.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.question: Which organisms are typically associated with periodontitis?
options: P. gingivalis, Tannerella forsythia, Aggregatibacter actinomycetemcomitans, Treponema denticola
Prevotella species, P. asaccharolytica, Fusobacterium species
items.explanation: The passage specifies that periodontitis is typically associated with P. gingivalis, Tannerella forsythia, Aggregatibacter actinomycetemcomitans, and Treponema denticola.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.question: What is a common clinical manifestation of anaerobic infections?
items.explanation: The passage lists abscess formation as a common clinical finding associated with anaerobic infections.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.question: Which condition can result from untreated pulpitis?
items.explanation: The passage explains that untreated pulpitis can progress to invade the alveolar bone and develop into a periapical abscess.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following anaerobic bacteria are most commonly isolated in chronic sinusitis?
items.explanation: Peptostreptococcus, Prevotella, and Porphyromonas species are the most commonly isolated anaerobes in chronic sinusitis.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.question: In which condition do anaerobic bacteria play a significant role?
items.explanation: Anaerobic bacteria are isolated in ~60% of cases of chronic suppurative otitis media in children.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.question: What is a common complication of anaerobic infections involving Lemierre's syndrome?
options: Septic thrombophlebitis of the internal jugular vein
items.explanation: Lemierre's syndrome involves septic thrombophlebitis of the internal jugular vein.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.question: Which CNS infection is most commonly associated with anaerobic bacteria due to contiguous spread?
items.explanation: Anaerobes are most commonly associated with brain abscesses resulting from contiguous spread.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.question: Which syndrome typically develops within 4–6 hours following anesthesia due to depressed gag reflex?
options: Chemical pneumonitis from foreign body inhalation
items.explanation: Mendelson syndrome results from regurgitation and aspiration of acidic gastric juices, typically developing within hours following anesthesia.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 3)

items.question: In bacterial aspiration pneumonia, where do chest x-rays show consolidation if the patient has aspirated while upright?
options: Basilar segments of the lower lobes
Posterior segment of the upper lobe
Superior segment of the lower lobe
items.explanation: Consolidation in bacterial aspiration pneumonia occurs in the basilar segments of the lower lobes if aspirated while upright.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 3)

items.question: Which organisms are most commonly isolated from anaerobic lung abscesses?
items.explanation: The most common isolates from anaerobic lung abscesses include peptostreptococci, Prevotella, and Porphyromonas species.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 3)

items.question: What is the typical time frame for constitutional symptoms to persist before hospitalization in anaerobic lung abscesses?
items.explanation: Constitutional symptoms typically persist for 1–3 weeks prior to hospitalization in anaerobic lung abscesses.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 3)

items.question: Which laboratory finding is most characteristic of empyema compared to parapneumonic effusion?
items.explanation: Empyema is characterized by a mean WBC count of ~55,000 cells/mL.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 4)

items.question: What is the typical pH level in empyema fluid?
items.explanation: Empyema fluid typically has a pH of <7.2.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 4)

items.question: Which organism is highly associated with severe disease and malignancy in neutropenic enterocolitis?
items.explanation: Clostridium septicum is highly associated with severe disease and malignancy in neutropenic enterocolitis.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 4)

items.question: Which anaerobic bacterial species are major pathogens in pelvic abscesses?
options: P. bivia, P. disiens, B. fragilis group
items.explanation: The major anaerobic pathogens in pelvic abscesses are P. bivia, P. disiens, and the B. fragilis group.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 4)

items.question: Which organism is most commonly associated with gas gangrene?
items.explanation: Gas gangrene (crepitus cellulitis) is most often due to C. perfringens.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 5)

items.question: What clinical feature is typical of progressive bacterial synergistic gangrene (Meleney gangrene)?
items.explanation: Progressive bacterial synergistic gangrene is characterized by an area of exquisite pain, redness, and swelling followed by ulceration surrounded by a violaceous ring.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 5)

items.question: Which type of necrotizing fasciitis progresses over 3–5 days with skin breakdown?
items.explanation: Polymicrobial necrotizing fasciitis differs from group A streptococcal necrotizing fasciitis in that the lesions progress over 3–5 days.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 5)

items.question: Which organism is typically associated with cervicofacial actinomycosis?
items.explanation: Actinomycotic involvement of the jaw was the most common bone infection, with Actinomyces species being implicated.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 5)

items.question: Which organism is most commonly isolated from blood cultures in cases of anaerobic bacteremia?
items.explanation: B. fragilis is the anaerobe most commonly isolated from blood cultures.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 6)

items.question: What is a common cause of mixed aerobic-anaerobic osteomyelitis?
items.explanation: Diabetic foot ulcers may be complicated by mixed aerobic-anaerobic osteomyelitis.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 6)

items.question: Which joints do Fusobacterium species have a predilection for in cases of anaerobic septic arthritis?
items.explanation: Fusobacterium species have a predilection for the sternoclavicular and sacroiliac joints.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 6)

items.question: What is the most common source of bacteremia in patients with B. fragilis?
items.explanation: The source of bacteremia is most often an abscess in the abdomen.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 6)

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a critical step in the diagnosis of anaerobic infection?
options: Rapid transport of the specimens to the microbiology laboratory, preferably in aerobic transport media.
Proper handling of the specimens by the laboratory.
Avoidance of contamination by normal microbiota during specimen collection.
items.explanation: Specimens should be transported in anaerobic, not aerobic, transport media.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which type of specimens is preferred for anaerobic culture?
options: Swab specimens from sites with normal microbiota.
Samples from the mouth, nose, and vagina.
items.explanation: Liquid or tissue specimens are preferred for anaerobic culture.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · DIAGNOSIS

items.question: What should be suspected if cultures yield no growth, streptococci only, or a single aerobic species but Gram's staining reveals a mixed bacterial population?
options: The presence of an aerobic infection.
Inadequate transport and/or culture techniques leading to failure in growing anaerobes.
A false-positive result due to contamination.
No further action is needed as the results are conclusive.
items.explanation: The involvement of anaerobes should be suspected if Gram's staining reveals a mixed population despite culture results suggesting otherwise.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: Anaerobic Infections | ANTIBIOTIC THERAPY AND RESISTANCE (part 1)
items.question: Which of the following antibiotics is NOT typically used as empirical therapy against anaerobic bacteria?
items.explanation: Aminoglycosides demonstrate no useful activity against anaerobes.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · Anaerobic Infections | ANTIBIOTIC THERAPY AND RESISTANCE (part 1)

items.subtopic: Anaerobic Infections | ANTIBIOTIC THERAPY AND RESISTANCE (part 1)
items.question: What is the primary reason for sending isolates to reference laboratories for susceptibility testing?
options: To confirm the presence of anaerobes
When an infection is serious or refractory
For routine monitoring in all cases
items.explanation: Isolates are sent to reference laboratories for susceptibility testing when an infection is serious, refractory, or requires prolonged therapy.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · Anaerobic Infections | ANTIBIOTIC THERAPY AND RESISTANCE (part 1)

items.subtopic: Anaerobic Infections | ANTIBIOTIC THERAPY AND RESISTANCE (part 1)
items.question: Which antibiotic has historically been effective against members of the B. fragilis group but now shows increased resistance rates?
items.explanation: Clindamycin was historically effective against members of the B. fragilis group, but resistance rates have increased.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · Anaerobic Infections | ANTIBIOTIC THERAPY AND RESISTANCE (part 1)

items.subtopic: Anaerobic Infections | ANTIBIOTIC THERAPY AND RESISTANCE (part 1)
items.question: Which statement best describes the role of antibiotic susceptibility testing for anaerobes?
options: It is routinely performed in most clinical microbiology laboratories.
It is limited due to slow growth rate and lack of standardized methods.
It is unnecessary because empirical therapy is always effective.
It should be avoided to prevent resistance development.
items.explanation: The role of antibiotic susceptibility testing for anaerobes has been limited in most clinical microbiology laboratories due to slow growth rate and lack of standardized methods.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · Anaerobic Infections | ANTIBIOTIC THERAPY AND RESISTANCE (part 1)

items.question: Which antibiotic has the lowest resistance rate among Bacteroides isolates?
items.explanation: Imipenem typically has the lowest resistance rate among carbapenems for Bacteroides isolates.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · ANTIBIOTIC THERAPY AND RESISTANCE (part 2)

items.question: Which antibiotic is not recommended as monotherapy due to high rates of resistance in the B. fragilis group?
items.explanation: Clindamycin is no longer recommended for intraabdominal infections involving anaerobes due to escalating resistance rates in the B. fragilis group.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · ANTIBIOTIC THERAPY AND RESISTANCE (part 2)

items.question: Which antibiotic combination is suitable for treating infections above the diaphragm that include a $\beta$-lactamase-producing organism?
items.explanation: A $\beta$-lactam/ $\beta$-lactamase inhibitor combination is recommended for infections above the diaphragm that include a $\beta$-lactamase-producing organism.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · ANTIBIOTIC THERAPY AND RESISTANCE (part 2)

items.question: Which antibiotic has poor activity against B. fragilis group?
items.explanation: Many fluoroquinolones exhibit poor activity against the B. fragilis group.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · ANTIBIOTIC THERAPY AND RESISTANCE (part 2)

items.subtopic: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms | HISTORIC PERSPECTIVE
items.statement: Anaerobes are not a predominant class of bacteria in the normal human microbiota.
items.explanation: Anaerobes constitute the predominant class of bacteria in the normal human microbiota.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms | HISTORIC PERSPECTIVE

items.subtopic: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms | HISTORIC PERSPECTIVE
items.statement: Leeuwenhoek used crushed pepper powder and clean rainwater as a culture medium.
items.explanation: Leeuwenhoek set up culture medium using crushed pepper powder and clean rainwater.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms | HISTORIC PERSPECTIVE

items.subtopic: DIFFERENCES BETWEEN ANAEROBIC AND AEROBIC ORGANISMS
items.statement: Microaerophilic organisms require high concentrations of oxygen for growth.
items.explanation: Microaerophilic organisms are damaged by atmospheric levels of oxygen but require low concentrations (2–10%) for growth.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · DIFFERENCES BETWEEN ANAEROBIC AND AEROBIC ORGANISMS

items.subtopic: DIFFERENCES BETWEEN ANAEROBIC AND AEROBIC ORGANISMS
items.statement: Fermentation is more efficient than aerobic respiration in terms of ATP production.
items.explanation: Fermentation results in ~5% of the energy released by aerobic respiration, making it less efficient.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · DIFFERENCES BETWEEN ANAEROBIC AND AEROBIC ORGANISMS

items.statement: Anaerobes make up less than 1% of the cultivable microbiota on human mucocutaneous surfaces.
items.explanation: The passage states that anaerobic bacteria often account for 99.0–99.9% of the cultivable microbiota.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · ANAEROBES OF THE HUMAN MICROBIOTA

items.statement: The Human Microbiome Project has characterized over 10,000 different bacterial species in the collective human microbiota.
items.explanation: The passage confirms that several projects have demonstrated the presence of >10,000 different bacterial species.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · ANAEROBES OF THE HUMAN MICROBIOTA

items.statement: The intestinal microbiota does not play a role in protecting against enteric infections.
items.explanation: The normal intestinal microbiota plays an important role in protection against enteric infections.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · ANAEROBES AND HUMAN HEALTH

items.statement: B. fragilis can modulate the immune system by inducing colonic regulatory T cells.
items.explanation: B. fragilis induces colonic regulatory T cells via expression of polysaccharide A (PSA).
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · ANAEROBES AND HUMAN HEALTH

items.statement: Anaerobic infections occur when there is a disruption between the host and its microbiota.
items.explanation: Anaerobic infections happen when the balance between the host and its microbiota is disturbed.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · ETIOLOGY

items.statement: The B. fragilis group contains fewer than 10 species.
items.explanation: The B. fragilis group encompasses 25 species, including B. thetaiotaomicron and B. vulgatus.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · ETIOLOGY

items.statement: P. gingivalis produces a collagenase that enhances tissue destruction.
items.explanation: Collagenase production by P. gingivalis contributes to its virulence and tissue destruction.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · ■ PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.statement: The capsular polysaccharide of B. fragilis is the least studied virulence factor among nonsporulating anaerobes.
items.explanation: The capsular polysaccharide complex of B. fragilis is one of the most extensively studied virulence factors.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · ■ PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: ■ PATHOGENESIS (part 2) | Infections Due to Anaerobic Bacteria
items.statement: Gas is diagnostic for anaerobic infections of deep tissues.
items.explanation: Gas can be produced by both aerobic and anaerobic bacteria, so it is not diagnostic.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · ■ PATHOGENESIS (part 2) | Infections Due to Anaerobic Bacteria

items.subtopic: ■ PATHOGENESIS (part 2) | Infections Due to Anaerobic Bacteria
items.statement: Manifestations of severe sepsis are common in patients with purely anaerobic infections.
items.explanation: Severe sepsis and disseminated intravascular coagulation are unusual in purely anaerobic infections.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · ■ PATHOGENESIS (part 2) | Infections Due to Anaerobic Bacteria

items.statement: Anaerobic bacteria account for more than 10% of all cases of bacteremia.
items.explanation: The passage states that anaerobic bacteria typically account for less than 1% of all cases of bacteremia.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.statement: Noma is most common in sub-Saharan Africa.
items.explanation: The passage indicates that noma occurs most frequently in sub-Saharan Africa.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.statement: Obligate anaerobes frequently cause meningitis.
items.explanation: Obligate anaerobes rarely cause meningitis, with very few cases reported in major studies.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.statement: Anaerobic bacteria are commonly involved in acute otitis media.
items.explanation: Anaerobic bacteria are not involved in acute otitis media.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.statement: Antibiotic therapy is indicated immediately upon diagnosis of chemical pneumonitis.
items.explanation: Antibiotic therapy is not indicated unless bacterial superinfection occurs in chemical pneumonitis.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 3)

items.statement: Empyema results from long-standing anaerobic pulmonary infection.
items.explanation: Empyema is a manifestation of long-standing anaerobic pulmonary infection.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 3)

items.statement: Intraabdominal infections often involve a limited repertoire of bacterial genera due to their propensity for causing abscesses and culturing difficulties.
items.explanation: The dominance of certain bacteria in intraabdominal infections is due to their tendency to cause abscesses and the challenges in culturing diverse organisms.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 4)

items.statement: Neutropenic enterocolitis typically presents with abdominal pain in all patients.
items.explanation: Abdominal pain may be absent in nearly half of cases of neutropenic enterocolitis.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 4)

items.statement: Fever is a typical symptom in progressive bacterial synergistic gangrene.
items.explanation: Symptoms of Meleney gangrene are limited to pain; fever is not typical.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 5)

items.statement: Actinomycosis commonly affects the maxilla more frequently than the mandible.
items.explanation: The mandible is involved four times as frequently as the maxilla in actinomycotic infections of the jaw.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 5)

items.statement: Hematogenous seeding of bone by anaerobes is common.
items.explanation: Hematogenous seeding of bone by anaerobes is uncommon and occurs in fewer than 10% of cases.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 6)

items.statement: Anaerobic endocarditis has a lower mortality rate compared to aerobic bacterial endocarditis.
items.explanation: Endocarditis due to gram-negative anaerobes is associated with significant mortality rates.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 6)

items.statement: Samples from sites known to harbor numerous anaerobes, such as the mouth and feces, are appropriate for anaerobic culture.
items.explanation: Such samples are not acceptable due to contamination by normal microbiota.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: Prior antibiotic therapy can reduce the cultivability of anaerobic bacteria.
items.explanation: Antibiotic treatment prior to culture can inhibit growth of anaerobes in cultures.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: Anaerobic Infections | ANTIBIOTIC THERAPY AND RESISTANCE (part 1)
items.statement: Metronidazole is effective against all anaerobic organisms.
items.explanation: Metronidazole is not clinically reliable against gram-positive nonspore-forming organisms and has resistance issues with some Bacteroides isolates.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · Anaerobic Infections | ANTIBIOTIC THERAPY AND RESISTANCE (part 1)

items.subtopic: Anaerobic Infections | ANTIBIOTIC THERAPY AND RESISTANCE (part 1)
items.statement: Rates of resistance to piperacillin-tazobactam for all Bacteroides species in the United States are notably high.
items.explanation: Resistance rates to piperacillin-tazobactam have remained low (≤1%) for all Bacteroides species in the United States.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · Anaerobic Infections | ANTIBIOTIC THERAPY AND RESISTANCE (part 1)

items.statement: Resistance to chloramphenicol is common among Bacteroides species.
items.explanation: Resistance to chloramphenicol is rare in Bacteroides species.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · ANTIBIOTIC THERAPY AND RESISTANCE (part 2)

items.statement: Cefotetan has good penetration into the CSF and can be used for CNS infections involving anaerobic organisms.
items.explanation: Cefotetan, like clindamycin, has poor penetration into the CSF and should not be used for CNS infections.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · ANTIBIOTIC THERAPY AND RESISTANCE (part 2)

items.subtopic: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms | HISTORIC PERSPECTIVE
items.assertion: Anaerobes cause disease when mucosal barriers break down.
items.reason: This allows the microbiota, including anaerobes, to enter normally sterile sites.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why anaerobes can cause disease when mucosal barriers break down.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms | HISTORIC PERSPECTIVE

items.subtopic: DIFFERENCES BETWEEN ANAEROBIC AND AEROBIC ORGANISMS
items.assertion: Anaerobic organisms can metabolize energy through fermentation.
items.reason: Fermentation releases energy from an organic molecule via substrate-level phosphorylation without using an electrochemical gradient.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why anaerobic organisms can use fermentation.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · DIFFERENCES BETWEEN ANAEROBIC AND AEROBIC ORGANISMS

items.assertion: The gut microbiota is implicated in the pathogenesis of diseases characterized by inflammation.
items.reason: Dysregulation of the gut microbiota may contribute to aberrant immune responses.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · ANAEROBES AND HUMAN HEALTH

items.assertion: Fusobacterium lipopolysaccharide contributes to the severity of Lemierre's syndrome.
items.reason: Lipopolysaccharides from Fusobacterium are highly active and lead to severe immune responses.
items.explanation: The reason correctly explains why Fusobacterium lipopolysaccharide contributes to the severity of Lemierre's syndrome.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · ■ PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.assertion: B. fragilis is rarely isolated from intraabdominal infections.
items.reason: It has a low presence in normal colonic microbiota.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; B. fragilis is commonly isolated from intraabdominal infections despite its low prevalence in the colonic microbiota.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · ■ PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.assertion: Acute necrotizing ulcerative gingivitis can progress to noma.
items.reason: Noma is a gangrenous infection that destroys oral cavity tissues and occurs in malnourished children with immune dysfunction.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains why acute necrotizing ulcerative gingivitis can progress to noma.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.assertion: Peritonsillar abscesses typically grow group A Streptococcus alone.
items.reason: The etiology of peritonsillar abscesses includes obligate anaerobes such as Bacteroides, Prevotella, and Peptostreptococcus species.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because peritonsillar abscesses grow group A Streptococcus in conjunction with obligate anaerobes.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.assertion: Anaerobic infections of the head and neck can lead to hematogenous complications.
items.reason: Bacteremia from anaerobic infections can result in endocarditis or other distant infections.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.assertion: Anaerobic bacteria are rarely recovered in cases of brain abscess.
items.reason: Brain abscesses are typically due to hematogenous seeding or recent surgical intervention.
items.explanation: The assertion is false as anaerobes can be commonly associated with brain abscesses from contiguous spread.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.assertion: Bacterial aspiration pneumonia develops over several days or weeks.
items.reason: The pathogenesis includes increased bacterial burden and potential airway damage from aspirated gastric fluid.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 3)

items.assertion: Sputum characteristically is malodorous in bacterial aspiration pneumonia.
items.reason: The process has been ongoing for at least a week.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; sputum is not malodorous unless the process has been ongoing for at least a week.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 3)

items.assertion: Anaerobic bacteria are frequently involved in pelvic inflammatory disease.
items.reason: Pelvic infections often involve both anaerobes and coliforms, with pure anaerobic infections more common in pelvic than intraabdominal sites.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 4)

items.assertion: Bacterial vaginosis is caused by an overgrowth of Lactobacillus species.
items.reason: The syndrome involves a change in bacterial ecology resulting in replacement of the normal microbiota with anaerobic bacteria.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; bacterial vaginosis involves a reduction, not overgrowth, of Lactobacillus species.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 4)

items.assertion: Necrotizing fasciitis caused by group A streptococci progresses more rapidly than polymicrobial necrotizing fasciitis.
items.reason: Group A streptococcal necrotizing fasciitis typically progresses over 1–3 days, whereas polymicrobial forms progress over 3–5 days.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 5)

items.assertion: Anaerobic bacteria are often cultured from foot ulcers in non-diabetic patients.
items.reason: Foot ulcers of diabetic patients commonly have anaerobic bacterial cultures.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the passage specifies that anaerobes are often cultured from foot ulcers of diabetic patients.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 5)

items.assertion: Isolation of a member of the B. fragilis group from blood indicates underlying infection.
items.reason: The presence of B. fragilis in blood cultures is associated with a mortality rate of 60% if untreated.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 6)

items.assertion: Anaerobic arthritis is commonly caused by multiple isolates.
items.reason: Anaerobic arthritis typically involves a single isolate.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, while the reason is true.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 6)

items.subtopic: Anaerobic Infections | ANTIBIOTIC THERAPY AND RESISTANCE (part 1)
items.assertion: Empirical therapy often achieves clinical resolution of anaerobic infections.
items.reason: Infections involving anaerobes are typically polymicrobial and not all organisms may be recovered.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why empirical antibiotics can achieve clinical resolution.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · Anaerobic Infections | ANTIBIOTIC THERAPY AND RESISTANCE (part 1)

items.subtopic: Anaerobic Infections | ANTIBIOTIC THERAPY AND RESISTANCE (part 1)
items.assertion: Aminoglycosides should be used as first-line therapy for anaerobic infections.
items.reason: Aminoglycosides demonstrate no useful activity against anaerobes.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because aminoglycosides are not recommended for anaerobic infections, but the reason is true.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · Anaerobic Infections | ANTIBIOTIC THERAPY AND RESISTANCE (part 1)

items.assertion: Clindamycin is no longer recommended as monotherapy for intraabdominal infections.
items.reason: The rates of resistance in the B. fragilis group have escalated.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why clindamycin is no longer recommended.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · ANTIBIOTIC THERAPY AND RESISTANCE (part 2)

items.assertion: Tigecycline has higher MICs for Clostridium species.
items.reason: Clostridium species are resistant to tigecycline.
items.explanation: While it is true that Tigecycline has higher MICs for Clostridium species, they are not resistant; thus, the reason does not correctly explain the assertion.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mixed Anaerobic Organisms · ANTIBIOTIC THERAPY AND RESISTANCE (part 2)
